title: The Association between Family Environment and Subsequent Risk of Cyberbullying Victimization in Adolescents.
abstract: BACKGROUND: Family environment and parental monitoring have long been recognized as two important factors associated with adolescents' psychological development. Studies have suggested a potential link between parenting style/parental engagement and the likelihood of bullying victimization among adolescents. Nonetheless, no studies to date have investigated the association between family environment and the subsequent risk of cyberbullying victimization among adolescents. In this study, we assessed the association between family environment (eg,Â parental monitoring and family conflict) and subsequent risk of cyberbullying victimization using data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD). METHODS: We used multivariable logistic regressions to assess the association between parental monitoring and family conflict at year 1 and the subsequent risk of cyberbullying victimization at year 2 in 10,410 eligible ABCD study participants. RESULTS: Adjusting for sociodemographic characteristics, study sampling weights and study site, higher levels of parental monitoring at year 1 were associated with a lower reported past 12-month (OR:Â 0.61, 95% CI: 0.50-0.75) history of cyberbullying victimization at year 2. Higher levels of family conflict at year 1 were associated with a higher risk of reported past 12-month history (OR:Â 1.10, 95% CI: 1.04-1.16) of cyberbullying victimization one year later. CONCLUSION: Higher levels of parental monitoring and lower levels of family conflict are associated with a subsequent lower risk of cyberbullying victimization among adolescents. Cyberbullying victimization preventive programs should advocate for increased parental monitoring and minimize family conflict at home to reduce the risks of cyberbullying victimization among adolescents.

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Multivariable logistic regression analyses. OR and 95% CI reported for associations between parental monitoring and family conflict at Year 1 and cyberbullying victimization at Year 2 (e.g., OR = 0.61, 95% CI 0.50–0.75 for parental monitoring; OR = 1.10, 95% CI 1.04–1.16 for family conflict).

Numeric extractable: OR, 95% CI, and p-values reported in Tables 2 and 3 for both mean scores and individual survey items. Example: parental monitoring OR = 0.61 (0.50, 0.75); family conflict OR = 1.10 (1.04, 1.16). Secondary analyses with individual items also include coefficients and CIs.

Original ABCD empirical analysis with prospective longitudinal design. Data collected at Year 1 (family environment measures) and Year 2 (cyberbullying victimization outcome), with 10,410 participants included in analyses.
Published article in Academic Pediatrics (2024); no evidence of duplication.

title: Prevalence and Patterns of Social Media Use in Early Adolescents.
abstract: OBJECTIVE: To describe patterns of social media use, including underage use (under 13 years) and sex differences, in a diverse, national sample of early adolescents in the US. METHODS: We analyzed the social media use data in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (2019-2021, Year 3), which includes a national sample of early adolescents in the US. Specifically, using Chi-squareÂ and t-tests, we compared social media use patterns across demographic characteristics stratified by age and sex. RESULTS: In the sample of 10,092 11-to-15-year-old adolescents, 69.5% had at least one social media account; among social media users, the most common platforms were TikTok (67.1%), YouTube (64.7%), and Instagram (66.0%). A majority (63.8%) of participants under 13 years (minimum age requirement) reported social media use. Under-13 social media users had an average of 3.38 social media accounts, with 68.2% having TikTok accounts and 39.0% saying TikTok was the social media site they used the most. Females reported higher use of TikTok, Snapchat, Instagram, and Pinterest, while males reported higher use of YouTube and Reddit. Additionally, 6.3% of participants with social media accounts reported having a secret social media account hidden from their parents' knowledge. CONCLUSIONS: Our findings reveal a high prevalence of underage social media use in early adolescence. These findings can inform current policies and legislation aimed at more robust age verification measures, minimum age requirements, and the enhancement of parental controls on social media. Clinicians can counsel about the potential risks of early adolescent social media use.

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Purely descriptive statistics. Paper reports prevalence percentages and Chi-square/t-tests for demographic stratification (age, sex) but NO inferential test linking SM variables to external outcome. No regression coefficient, correlation, or statistical test of SM predicting a non-SM outcome. Per B10—SM is only characterized descriptively, not tested against external outcome.
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: Chi-square tests and t-tests for comparisons across demographic characteristics; descriptive proportions and statistical tests comparing groups by age and sex

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: No regression coefficients, OR, RR, HR, d, r, F, t, χ², η², R², p-values, or CI reported for SM↔outcome associations. Only descriptive percentages and prevalence data in tables.
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: Percentages, proportions, mean hours/day, Chi-square p-values, and t-test p-values reported in Table 1 and text

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD Study data using descriptive methods (Chi-square, t-tests) on cross-sectional Year 3 follow-up data.
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: Pure descriptive/prevalence study; no SM↔non-SM-outcome analysis. Applies B14: descriptive data collection on platform use and prevalence patterns only. No inferential test of SM predicting an external outcome.

title: The association between screen time and cognitive function in children: The partially mediating role of putamen volume.
abstract: OBJECTIVE: This study investigates the association between screen time and cognitive function and examines whether putamen volume, which decreases with increased screen time, mediates this relationship. METHODS: Using data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study of nearly 10,000 children aged 9-10Â years, screen time was assessed through self-report questionnaires, and cognitive function-including working memory, processing speed, language, executive function, and episodic memory-was Æmeasured using the National Institutes of Health toolbox. Linear mixed-effects models were used to analyze the association between screen time and cognitive function, and mediation effects of putamen volume were evaluated. RESULTS: Screen time was significantly negatively associated with all domains of cognitive function. The volume of putamen partially mediated the relationship between screen time and executive function (Î²Â =Â -0.015, false discovery rate [FDR] pÂ =Â .027), processing speed (Î²Â =Â -0.012, FDR pÂ =Â .027), and language (Î²Â =Â -0.005, FDR pÂ =Â .027). CONCLUSION: Excessive screen time was negatively associated with cognitive function in children, with putamen volume involving in the association of screen time with executive function, language and processing speed. These findings could inform strategies to promote healthy screen habits and support cognitive function and brain development in children.

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Screen time (total and by digital device: television, videos, video games, texting, video chat) as independent variable. Meets B1 (total screen time reported; also examined by device). Applies B1 rule.
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: Screen time measured as total aggregate across unspecified platforms (television, videos, video games, texting, video chat) without separate per-item coefficients in main analyses. Main results report only total screen time β; represents internet use without device/platform specificity per B5. Fails B2 (only summed/total screen time, no per-item breakdown in main results).
